5: Israel's demand: śîmāh lānû melek lᵉšāpəṭēnû kəḵol haggôyim — 'give us a king to judge us like all the nations.' The phrase kəḵol haggôyim (like all the nations) is the demand's theological indictment: Israel's covenant identity is precisely its differentiation from the nations. Deut 17:14-20 anticipated this moment and regulated kingship — kings were permitted but must not multiply horses (military alliance with Egypt), wives (foreign treaties), or silver and gold (autonomous wealth). The demand here goes beyond the Deuteronomic provision: they want a king not to embody covenant rule but to replace YHWH as the source of security and governance. Lᵉšāpəṭēnû (to judge us) — the same verb šāpaṭ used for the judges — signals that they want a permanent institutional judge, not the crisis-charismatic judges YHWH has been providing.
7: YHWH's verdict to Samuel: kî lōʾ ʾōtəḵā māʾāsû kî ʾōtî māʾāsû mimᵉlōḵ ʿălêhem — 'for it is not you they have rejected, but me they have rejected from being king over them.' The verb māʾas (to reject, despise) is the same word used in 15:23 and 15:26 when YHWH rejects Saul. The rejection is mutual and sequential: Israel rejects YHWH as king; YHWH will later reject Saul as the king Israel chose. The theological structure is precise: melek ʿălêhem (king over them) — YHWH's kingship is the real category at stake. The entire book of 1 Samuel is framed by two rejections: Israel rejects YHWH's kingship in ch8; YHWH rejects Saul's kingship in ch15. Between them is the question of what kind of king YHWH will provide — which chs 16-31 and the entire Davidic tradition answer.
9: Samuel is commanded to warn Israel of the mišpaṭ hammelek — 'the manner/justice of the king.' The word mišpaṭ normally refers to covenant justice — YHWH's justice, the standard by which leaders are to be measured. Its use here for royal taxation and conscription is ironic: the 'justice' of a human king is coercive extraction, the opposite of the mišpaṭ YHWH's covenant produces. The list (vv11-17): sons taken for the chariot corps and harvest work, daughters for perfumers and cooks, fields and vineyards taken, a ten-percent tithe to royal servants — is the exact catalog of Solomonic administrative excess documented in 1 Kgs 4-5. The warning Samuel gives is the structural analysis of kingship that will be precisely fulfilled in the Solomonic monarchy.
1: Vayehi ki-zaqen Shemu’el vayasem et-banav shoftim le-Yisra’el (“when Samuel grew old, he appointed his sons as judges over Israel”). The appointment of sons as successors repeats the pattern of Eli (corrupt sons). Yasem (hiphil of sum: to set/appoint) is the standard appointment verb. The dynastic impulse — appointing family members rather than gifted successors — is identified as a structural vulnerability of the pre-monarchic arrangement. Samuel’s sons will fail as judges; this failure becomes the proximate cause of the monarchic demand.
2: The firstborn was named Joel (Yo’el, “YHWH is God”) and the second Abijah (Aviyah, “YHWH is my father”). Both names are theophoric — containing the divine name. The irony: sons with names declaring YHWH’s sovereignty and fatherhood will repudiate both. They served as judges at Beersheba (ba-Beer-sheva’) — the southernmost significant site, far from their father’s home circuit (Bethel, Gilgal, Mizpah).
3: Velo halekhu vanav bidrachav vayyitu achar ha-batza’ vayiqechu shochad vayyattu mishpat (“his sons did not follow his example — they turned to greed, accepted bribes, and twisted justice”). The three-part corruption catalog: batza’ (unjust gain/greed), shochad (bribe), and nattah mishpat (perverting justice). Batza’ is from baza’ (to cut off, plunder) — exploiting the judicial position for financial benefit. Shochad is specifically bribery in judicial contexts (condemned in Exod 23:8; Deut 16:19; 27:25; Isa 1:23; Ezek 22:12). Nattah mishpat (turn/bend justice) is the standard idiom for perverting legal outcomes (Exod 23:6; Deut 16:19).
4: Vayit’qabetzu kol ziqnei Yisra’el vayavo’u el Shemu’el ha-Ramathah (“all the elders of Israel assembled and came to Samuel at Ramah”). The gathering of ziqnei Yisra’el (elders of Israel) is the constitutional procedure for national decision-making. The elders represent the tribal-clan leadership structure that predates the monarchy. Their coming to Samuel at Ramah echoes the earlier scene of Hannah coming to the sanctuary for divine intervention — a structural parallel that highlights how far Israel has drifted from Hannah’s posture of dependence.
6: Vayera’ ha-davar be-einei Shemu’el ka-asher ameru tenu-lanu melekh leshoftenu (“the request displeased Samuel when they said, ‘Give us a king to judge us’”). The phrase vayera’ be-einav (it was evil/displeasing in his eyes) uses the standard Deuteronomistic evaluative formula. Ka-asher ameru tenu-lanu melekh leshoftenu — the infinitive leshoftenu (to judge us) qualifies the request: they want the king specifically in the judicial-governance role that Samuel has occupied. Vayitpallel Shemu’el el YHWH (“Samuel prayed to the LORD”): prayer is Samuel’s first response, consistent with his character throughout.
8: YHWH’s explanation of the request: kekhal-ha-ma’asim asher asu meha-yom asher he’elitim mi-Mitzrayim ve-ad ha-yom hazeh vaya’azvuni vaya’avdu elohim acherim (“this is consistent with everything they have done since the day I brought them up out of Egypt... forsaking me and serving other gods”). The phrase ken hemmah osim gam-lakh (“now they are doing the same to you”) places Samuel’s personal rejection in the context of Israel’s long history of rejecting YHWH. This contextualizes the request as apostasy-pattern rather than merely pragmatic politics.
10: Vayidabber Shemu’el et kol divrei YHWH el ha-am hasha’alim me-ito melekh (“Samuel reported all the LORD’s words to the people who had asked him for a king”). Hasha’alim (those who asked/requested) uses the same sha’al root as the name Samuel and the name Saul. The verb echoes throughout the chapter: the people sha’al for a king; YHWH says give them what they sha’al for; the king who results will be named Sha’ul. The entire king-request is lexically bound to the asking/requesting vocabulary.
11: The mishpat ha-melekh (“the law/practice of the king”) begins here. Mishpat can mean both “legal right” (what the king has the legal right to do) or “customary practice” (what kings normally do). The ambiguity is intentional: this is both what the king will do and what he has the right to do under royal custom. The ANE parallels are striking: Mesopotamian royal inscriptions detail the king’s rights of corvée labor, military conscription, and taxation of produce. Samuel’s speech echoes these real royal prerogatives. Yiqach et-beneikhem (“he will take your sons”): the fourfold yiqach (he will take) in vv. 11-17 creates a rhetorical hammer of royal appropriation.
12: The military and agricultural drafts: commanders (sarim) over thousands and fifties, sons for plowing (lachrosh charish), harvesting (liqtzor qatziro), and weapons-making (la’asot kelei milchamto ukeli rikkvo, “his war equipment and chariot gear”). The military hierarchy (commanders of thousands and fifties, alfav va-chamishav) is the standard Israelite military unit organization (Exod 18:21; Deut 1:15). The royal economy requires both fighting capacity and agricultural production to sustain it.
13: Daughters taken to serve as raqqachot ve-tabbachot ve-ophot (perfumers, cooks, bakers). The three roles are women’s palace crafts — raqqach (perfumer) appears in Exod 30:25, 35 for the sacred anointing oil and incense makers. The palace economy requires female specialists in aromatic preparation, food preparation, and baking. The inclusion of daughters alongside sons in the royal drafts equalizes the exploitation across gender.
14: Ve-et sadoteikhem ve-kharmakhem ve-zeitei teivkhem ha-tovim yiqach (“he will seize your best fields, vineyards, and olive groves”). The three agricultural assets — grain fields, vineyards, olive groves — are the complete Mediterranean agricultural economy (also the three commodities of Deut 8:8’s seven-species abundance). Ha-tovim (the best/finest) — the king takes not a portion but the best portion. The distribution to his officials (ve-natan la-avedav) turns the royal appropriation into a patronage system.
15: Ve-zar’eikhem ve-kharmakhem ya’aser (“he will tithe your grain and your vineyards”). The royal ma’aser (tithe) is set at one-tenth — the same rate as the Levitical tithe (Num 18:21-24). This is deliberate: the royal tithe competes with YHWH’s tithe for the same agricultural produce. The monarch becomes a tithe-collector for his own officers (sarisav ulevadav), parallel to YHWH’s tithe supporting the Levites. The rhetorical point: the king takes from Israel what belongs to YHWH and directs it to his own household.
16: Ve-et-avdeikhem ve-et-amhotekhem ve-et-bachurekhem ha-tovim (“he will take your male and female servants, your finest young men”). The addition of donkeys (ve-et-chamorekhem) to servants creates a rhetorical descent: human servants and draft animals are equivalently absorbed into the royal service economy. Ve-asah l’malachto (“put them to work for himself”) — the royal monopolization of labor is total: the agricultural, domestic, and animal resources of the people become the king’s productive capital.
17: Tzone’ikhem ya’aser ve-atem tihyu lo la-avadim (“he will take a tenth of your flocks, and you will become his subjects”). The climax: from taking portions of produce and labor, the king ends by taking a tenth of the flocks and reducing the people to avadim (servants/slaves). The word avadim (servants) is loaded: it is the term for Israel in Egyptian bondage (Exod 1:13-14: vaya’avidu Mitzrayim et benei Yisra’el be-pharekh, the Egyptians enslaved them with hard labor). The monarchy Samuel warns against will reproduce the slavery of Egypt.
18: Uzzeqaqtem bayyom hahu mipnei malkkhem (“in that day you will cry out because of the king you have chosen for yourselves”). Zeqaq (cry out) is the verb of distress used for Israel’s cry in Egypt (Exod 2:23; 14:10) and echoed throughout Samuel (7:8-9). The bitter irony: Israel wanted a king to stop crying out under Philistine oppression; the king will cause them to cry out instead. Ve-lo ya’aneh YHWH etkhem bayyom hahu (“but the LORD will not answer you”) — the standard divine-response formula negated. YHWH’s refusal to answer the cry they create for themselves is the consequence of their rejection of his kingship.
19: Vayema’anu ha-am lishmo’a be-qol Shemu’el vayomru lo ki im-melekh yihyeh alenu (“but the people refused to listen to Samuel and said, ‘No! We will have a king over us’”). Ma’en (refuse, niphal of ma’an) is the refusal verb of Pharaoh (Exod 7:14; 8:2; 9:2; 10:3-4): ma’en... leshalach (“refuses to let go”). Israel’s refusal to listen to Samuel mirrors Pharaoh’s refusal to listen to Moses. The people who were freed from Pharaoh’s ma’en now themselves ma’en to follow YHWH.
20: Vehayinu gam-anachnu ke-khol ha-goyim (“so that we may be like all the other nations”). This is the theological heart of the chapter: Israel’s desire to be ke-khol ha-goyim (like all the nations). The Sinai covenant established Israel as YHWH’s distinctive (segullah) people, a kingdom of priests, a holy nation (Exod 19:5-6) — not like the nations. The request to be ke-khol ha-goyim is the formal rejection of the covenant’s distinctiveness. The king they want: to “rule us, lead us out, and fight our battles” (ushefatanu u-vatza’ lefaneinu ve-nilcham et-milchamoteinu) — the military leadership function they attribute to the king is precisely what YHWH provided as divine warrior throughout the judges period.
21: Samuel heard all the words (kol divrei ha-am) and repeated them before YHWH (vayedabberem be-oznei YHWH). The verb dabar be-oznei (speak in the ears of) is used for official transmission of important communication (Exod 17:14; Gen 50:4). Samuel’s role here is as messenger relaying the people’s formal position to YHWH — the intercessory function in reverse: rather than carrying YHWH’s word to the people (cf. 8:10), he carries the people’s word to YHWH.
22: YHWH’s final word: shema bequolam (“listen to their voice”) and vehemlekhta lahem melekh (“give them a king”). Divine consent given to human desire does not constitute divine approval: this is the grammar of judgment through permission (cf. Rom 1:24, 26, 28: God “gave them over”). YHWH grants the monarchy knowing it will ultimately fail and lead to the captivity. Samuel’s final instruction — lekhu ish le-iro (“each of you, return to your town”) — disperses the assembly without ceremony. The momentous decision to establish a monarchy is registered in the departure formula of dismissed troops.
